10.1016/j.jmp.2024.102840
Knowledge Structure Theory (KST), initially developed by  Doignon and Falmagne (1985, 1999) , has undergone significant advancements by other researchers ( Falmagne et al., 2013; Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 ). Originally focused on dichotomous items, KST has been extended to incorporate items with multiple response alternatives.

Schrepp (1997)  generalized KST to handle items with multiple response alternatives, while  Stefanutti et al. (2020)  established a robust mathematical foundation for extending KST to items with more than two ordered response categories using linearly ordered sets and complete lattices.  Heller (2021)  further extended quasi-ordinal knowledge spaces to polytomous items by incorporating partially ordered response values into lattices.  Stefanutti et al. (2023)  developed an axiomatic theory of attribute maps, extending skill maps to polytomous knowledge structures and establishing a deterministic relationship between attributes and observable item responses. These advancements have significantly advanced the understanding and application of KST, offering diverse mathematical frameworks and perspectives for assessing knowledge across various contexts. This expansion has also sparked substantial research dedicated to exploring polytomous KST.

In the realm of polytomous knowledge structures,  Wang et al. (2023)  and  Ge (2022)  established Galois connections between polytomous knowledge structures and polytomous attribute functions. However, due to differing interpretations, their findings yielded distinct closed elements. Specifically, Wang et al. focused on component-directed polytomous knowledge spaces, contrasting with  Stefanutti et al. (2020) ’s more rigorous concept of polytomous knowledge spaces by adopting a more flexible criterion for the join operation. Moreover,  Sun, Li, He, et al. (2023)  contributed to the understanding of polytomous knowledge structures by extending the notion of well-gradedness to the polytomous case.

The integration of fuzzy mathematics and polytomous KST has brought about significant advancements.  Bartl and Belohlavek (2011)  proposed using fuzzy sets to represent knowledge states, with membership degrees indicating individual mastery levels.  Zhou et al. (2022)  focused on constructing personalized learning paths through polytomous structures based on item and fuzzy skill relationships.  Sun, Li, Lin, and He (2023)  developed specialized algorithms for constructing polytomous knowledge structures, exploring the connection between fuzzy skills and polytomous items.

Turning our attention to well-graded families in the field of knowledge structures, the concept of well-graded knowledge structures was initially introduced and refined by  Doignon and Falmagne (1997)  in the finite case. This exploration of well-gradedness stemmed from a stochastic theory developed by  Falmagne and Doignon (1988) , which has implications for the development of efficient algorithms for knowledge assessment ( Eppstein et al., 2009 ).  Cosyn and Uzun (2009)  further demonstrated the equivalence between learning spaces and well-graded knowledge spaces.  Matayoshi (2017)  also examined various properties of well-graded union-closed families that exclude the empty set. In addition to their theoretical properties, well-graded families hold practical significance and serve as the foundation for artificial intelligence systems such as the ALEKS system ( Falmagne et al., 2006; Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 ).

In the field of polytomous KST, there have been initial advancements in the study of well-graded structures.  Heller (2021)  introduced a metric  
                         
                            
                               δ 
                            
                            
                               p 
                            
                         
                      , on a finite graded lattice, which enables the measurement of distance  
                         δ 
                       between polytomous knowledge states. Expanding on this metric,  Sun, Li, He, et al. (2023)  utilized it to define and explore the concept of well-gradedness in the polytomous case. It is worth noting that  Ovchinnikov (2002)  proposed an alternative concept of well-gradedness, which exhibits conciseness and certain geometric properties when applied to finite linear orders.

This paper adopts a rigorous approach to the subject matter and builds upon the works of  Sun, Li, He, et al. (2023)  and  Ovchinnikov (2002) , aiming to provide a comprehensive understanding of well-graded families in the context of polytomous KST. By thoroughly analyzing the definition of well-gradedness proposed by Sun et al. we offer an extensive overview of this concept. Additionally, we propose an equivalent characterization of well-graded families based on the insights provided by Ovchinnikov. Furthermore, we explore various manifestations of well-gradedness in polytomous knowledge structures, including the examination of strong discriminative properties, the representation of well-gradedness in polytomous knowledge spaces, and the well-gradedness property of discriminative factorial polytomous knowledge structures. Through the research, our aim is to contribute to the advancement and deeper understanding of this critical aspect within the field of Knowledge Structure Theory.

The remaining sections of the paper are organized as follows: In Section  2 , we introduce key concepts in KST and discuss the extension of KST to incorporate polytomous items. Section  3  presents our proposed characterization of well-gradedness in a general non-empty family. Moving forward, in Section  4 , we delve into the examination of various manifestations of well-gradedness specifically in polytomous knowledge structures. Finally, in Section  5 , we summarize the main findings and contributions of our research.

In this section, we provide a concise overview of the fundamental concepts in Knowledge Structure Theory and polytomous KST. For a more comprehensive understanding, we suggest referring to authoritative sources such as  Falmagne and Doignon (2011) ,  Stefanutti et al. (2020) , and  Heller (2021) .

Let  
                            Q 
                          be a nonempty set of items, and let  
                            K 
                          be a family of subsets of  
                            Q 
                         .  Doignon and Falmagne (1985)  introduced the concept of a  knowledge structure , denoted as  
                            
                               ( 
                               Q 
                               , 
                               K 
                               ) 
                            
                         , where  
                            K 
                          contains at least  
                            Q 
                          and the empty set  
                            0̸ 
                         . In this context,  
                            Q 
                          represents the  domain  of the knowledge structure, and the elements  
                            K 
                          in  
                            K 
                          are referred to as  knowledge states . If an item  
                            q 
                          belongs to a knowledge state  
                            K 
                         , it indicates that an individual in that state has completely mastered the item  
                            q 
                         . An  atom  at  
                            q 
                          is a defined as a minimal set in  
                            K 
                          that contains  
                            q 
                         . In other words, it is the smallest subset of  
                            Q 
                          in  
                            K 
                          that includes  
                            q 
                         . A set  
                            
                               B 
                               ∈ 
                               K 
                            
                          is called an  atom  if it is an atom at  
                            q 
                          for some  
                            
                               q 
                               ∈ 
                               Q 
                            
                         . This means that  
                            B 
                          is a minimal knowledge state that includes a specific item  
                            q 
                          and does not contain any other proper subset of  
                            B 
                          that also includes  
                            q 
                         .

A knowledge structure is classified as  discriminative  if for any two distinct items  
                            p 
                          and  
                            q 
                         , there exists a state  
                            K 
                          that can only contain one of these items. A knowledge structure  
                            K 
                          is considered a  knowledge space  if it satisfies the property of being closed under union. This means that if  
                            K 
                          and  
                            L 
                          are both states of  
                            K 
                         , then their union  
                            
                               K 
                               ∪ 
                               L 
                            
                          is also a state of  
                            K 
                         . Additionally, if the knowledge space  
                            K 
                          is closed under intersection, it is referred to as a  quasi-ordinal space .  Birkhoff (1937)  established a one-to-one correspondence between the family of all quasi-ordinal spaces  
                            K 
                          on a domain  
                            Q 
                          and the family of all quasi-orders  
                            ≺ 
                          on  
                            Q 
                         .

To facilitate comparison and discussion in subsequent sections, we have selected relevant conclusions from the existing literature regarding the well-gradedness property. Specifically,  Falmagne and Doignon (2011)  and  Ovchinnikov (2011)  have provided the following equivalent conditions for a non-empty family  
                            
                               K 
                               ⊆ 
                               
                                  
                                     2

                            K

                               n

                            K 
                          and  
                            L

                            K 
                          is equal to 1. Two distinct elements  
                            K 
                          and  
                            L 
                          are adjacent in  
                            K

                               K 
                               ∩ 
                               L 
                               ⊆ 
                               M 
                               ⊆ 
                               K

                               L

                               M

                               K

                               M

                               L 
                            
                         .

In knowledge spaces,  Koppen (1998)  first identified the following characteristic:

A knowledge space  
                            K 
                          is considered well-graded if and only if each atom in  
                            K 
                          corresponds to a unique item. This property guarantees that the family  
                            
                               { 
                               
                                  
                                     B 
                                  
                                  
                                     q 
                                  
                               
                               ∣ 
                               q 
                               ∈ 
                               Q 
                               } 
                            
                          forms a partition of  
                            B 
                         , where  
                            
                               
                                  B 
                               
                               
                                  q 
                               
                            
                          refers to the set of atoms associated with item  
                            q 
                         , and  
                            B 
                          represents the set of all atoms.

Additionally, any finite discriminative quasi-ordinal space is well-graded (e.g.,  Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 ).

These conclusions establish the relationship between the well- gradedness property and the atoms of knowledge spaces and highlight the applicability of the well-graded concept to discriminative quasi-ordinal spaces. However, it should be noted that while most of these conclusions can be extended to polytomous KST, there are also instances where the well-gradedness property may not hold. These exceptional cases will be discussed in the final Conclusions section.

                               ( 
                               X 
                               , 
                               ≤

                                     ′

                               c

                            X

                            C

                                  | 
                                  C 
                                  |

                            1

In a partially ordered set (poset)  
                            X 
                         , an upper bound of a subset  
                            Y 
                          is an element  
                            
                               x 
                               ∈ 
                               X 
                            
                          satisfying  
                            
                               y 
                               ≤ 
                               x 
                            
                          for all  
                            
                               y 
                               ∈ 
                               Y 
                            
                         . A least upper bound of  
                            Y 
                          is an upper bound  
                            x 
                          of  
                            Y 
                          such that every other upper bound  
                            z 
                          of  
                            Y 
                          satisfies  
                            
                               x 
                               ≤ 
                               z 
                            
                         . When the least upper bound of a pair of elements  
                            
                               { 
                               x 
                               , 
                               y 
                               } 
                            
                          exists, it is denoted as  
                            
                               x 
                               ∨ 
                               y 
                            
                          (read as “ 
                            x 
                          join  
                            y 
                         ” or “ 
                            x 
                          sup  
                            y 
                         ”). Similarly, the greatest lower bound  
                            
                               x 
                               ∧ 
                               y 
                            
                          (read as “ 
                            x 
                          meet  
                            y 
                         ” or “ 
                            x 
                          inf  
                            y 
                         ”) is defined when it exists. A poset  
                            X 
                          is called a  lattice  if every pair of elements in  
                            X 
                          has both a join and a meet. In other words, for any elements  
                            
                               a 
                               , 
                               b 
                               ∈ 
                               X 
                            
                         , there exist elements  
                            
                               x 
                               = 
                               a 
                               ∨ 
                               b 
                            
                          (join) and  
                            
                               y 
                               = 
                               a 
                               ∧ 
                               b 
                            
                          (meet) in  
                            X 
                         . A finite lattice  
                            X 
                          is said to be  modular  if, for any elements  
                            
                               a 
                               , 
                               b 
                               , 
                               c 
                               ∈ 
                               X 
                            
                          with  
                            
                               a 
                               ≤ 
                               c 
                            
                         , the following equality holds:  
                            
                               c 
                               ∧ 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  b 
                                  ∨ 
                                  a 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  c 
                                  ∧ 
                                  b 
                                  ) 
                               
                               ∨ 
                               a 
                            
                         . These definitions are commonly used in lattice theory and have been discussed in detail by  Davey and Priestley (2002) .

Fig. 1  illustrates two common examples of finite modular lattices. In  Fig. 1(a) , the elements in set  
                            X 
                          are arranged in a linear order:  
                            
                               a 
                               ≤ 
                               b 
                               ≤ 
                               c 
                               ≤ 
                               d 
                            
                         . This linear order implies that every pair of elements in  
                            X 
                          has a clear hierarchy of precedence. In  Fig. 1(b) , the elements in set  
                            X 
                          form a general lattice structure with the partial order  
                            
                               a 
                               ≤ 
                               b 
                               ≤ 
                               d 
                            
                          and  
                            
                               a 
                               ≤ 
                               c 
                               ≤ 
                               d 
                            
                         . Notably, the elements  
                            b 
                          and  
                            c 
                          are incomparable within the lattice, meaning that there is no defined precedence between them. Instead, they exist as separate branches stemming from the common ancestor  
                            a 
                         .

An alternative definition for a finite modular lattice is as follows: A finite lattice  
                            X 
                          considered modular if and only if it is graded and its rank function  
                            ρ 
                          satisfies the equation:  
                            
                               (1) 
                               
                                  
                                     ρ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        x 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     + 
                                     ρ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        y 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     = 
                                     ρ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        x 
                                        ∧ 
                                        y 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     + 
                                     ρ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        x 
                                        ∨ 
                                        y 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     , 
                                  
                               
                            
                         for all  
                            
                               x 
                               , 
                               y 
                            
                          in  
                            X 
                          (refer to Section 3.3 in ( Stanley, 2011 ) for details). In this case, the metric  
                            δ 
                          on  
                            X 
                          is defined as:  
                            
                               (2) 
                               
                                  
                                     δ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        x 
                                        , 
                                        y 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     = 
                                     ρ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        x 
                                        ∨ 
                                        y 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     − 
                                     ρ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        x 
                                        ∧ 
                                        y 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     , 
                                  
                               
                            
                         for all  
                            
                               x 
                               , 
                               y 
                            
                          in  
                            X 
                          (see Chapter 8 in  Rutherford, 1965 ). However, it is important to note that if a graded lattice is not modular, then the corresponding rank function may not necessarily satisfy Eq.  (1) . Therefore, even if the metric  
                            δ 
                          is constructed as described in Eq.  (2) , it may not possess the necessary properties to be considered a valid metric on  
                            X 
                         . It is crucial to ensure that the rank function satisfies Eq.  (1)  in order to establish the desired properties of the metric.

Example 2.1 
                            Consider the graded lattice  
                                  
                                     X 
                                     = 
                                     
                                        { 
                                        a 
                                        , 
                                        b 
                                        , 
                                        c 
                                        , 
                                        d 
                                        , 
                                        e 
                                        , 
                                        f 
                                        } 
                                     
                                  
                                with the partial order defined as  
                                  
                                     a 
                                     ≤ 
                                     b 
                                     ≤ 
                                     d 
                                     ≤ 
                                     f 
                                  
                                and  
                                  
                                     a 
                                     ≤ 
                                     c 
                                     ≤ 
                                     e 
                                     ≤ 
                                     f 
                                  
                               . In this partial order, the elements in the set  
                                  
                                     { 
                                     b 
                                     , 
                                     d 
                                     }

                                     { 
                                     c 
                                     , 
                                     e 
                                     } 
                                  
                                (see  Fig. 2 ). The rank function  
                                  
                                     ρ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        x 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                                represents the length of a maximal chain between  
                                  a 
                                and  
                                  x 
                               . It is easy to verify that  
                                  
                                     ρ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        b 
                                        )

                                     ρ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        c 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     = 
                                     2 
                                  
                                and  
                                  
                                     ρ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        b 
                                        ∨ 
                                        c 
                                        )

                                     ρ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        b 
                                        ∧ 
                                        c 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     = 
                                     3 
                                  
                               , which does not satisfy  (1) . This indicates that the lattice  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     X 
                                     , 
                                     ≤ 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                is not modular. Let  
                                  
                                     δ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        x 
                                        , 
                                        y 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                                be defined as in Eq.  (2) . In this case, we have  
                                  
                                     δ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        b 
                                        , 
                                        c 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     = 
                                     ρ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        b 
                                        ∨ 
                                        c 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     − 
                                     ρ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        b 
                                        ∧ 
                                        c 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     = 
                                     3 
                                  
                               ,  
                                  
                                     δ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        b 
                                        , 
                                        a 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     = 
                                     1 
                                  
                               , and  
                                  
                                     δ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        a 
                                        , 
                                        c 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     = 
                                     1 
                                  
                               . Therefore,  
                                  
                                     δ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        b 
                                        , 
                                        c 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     > 
                                     δ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        b 
                                        , 
                                        a 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     + 
                                     δ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        a 
                                        , 
                                        c 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                               . As a result, the metric  
                                  δ 
                                does not satisfy the triangle inequality, which is a fundamental property of metrics. Hence,  
                                  δ 
                                cannot be considered a valid metric on  
                                  X 
                               .

Consider the graded lattice  
                                  
                                     X 
                                     = 
                                     
                                        { 
                                        a 
                                        , 
                                        b 
                                        , 
                                        c 
                                        , 
                                        d 
                                        , 
                                        e 
                                        , 
                                        f 
                                        } 
                                     
                                  
                                with the partial order defined as  
                                  
                                     a 
                                     ≤ 
                                     b 
                                     ≤ 
                                     d 
                                     ≤ 
                                     f 
                                  
                                and  
                                  
                                     a 
                                     ≤ 
                                     c 
                                     ≤ 
                                     e 
                                     ≤ 
                                     f 
                                  
                               . In this partial order, the elements in the set  
                                  
                                     { 
                                     b 
                                     , 
                                     d 
                                     } 
                                  
                                are incomparable with the elements in the set  
                                  
                                     { 
                                     c 
                                     , 
                                     e 
                                     } 
                                  
                                (see  Fig. 2 ). The rank function  
                                  
                                     ρ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        x 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                                represents the length of a maximal chain between  
                                  a 
                                and  
                                  x 
                               . It is easy to verify that  
                                  
                                     ρ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        b 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     + 
                                     ρ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        c 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     = 
                                     2 
                                  
                                and  
                                  
                                     ρ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        b 
                                        ∨ 
                                        c 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     + 
                                     ρ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        b 
                                        ∧ 
                                        c 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     = 
                                     3 
                                  
                               , which does not satisfy  (1) . This indicates that the lattice  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     X 
                                     , 
                                     ≤ 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                is not modular. Let  
                                  
                                     δ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        x 
                                        , 
                                        y 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                                be defined as in Eq.  (2) . In this case, we have  
                                  
                                     δ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        b 
                                        , 
                                        c 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     = 
                                     ρ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        b 
                                        ∨ 
                                        c 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     − 
                                     ρ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        b 
                                        ∧ 
                                        c 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     = 
                                     3 
                                  
                               ,  
                                  
                                     δ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        b 
                                        , 
                                        a 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     = 
                                     1 
                                  
                               , and  
                                  
                                     δ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        a 
                                        , 
                                        c 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     = 
                                     1 
                                  
                               . Therefore,  
                                  
                                     δ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        b 
                                        , 
                                        c 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     > 
                                     δ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        b 
                                        , 
                                        a 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     + 
                                     δ 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        a 
                                        , 
                                        c 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                               . As a result, the metric  
                                  δ 
                                does not satisfy the triangle inequality, which is a fundamental property of metrics. Hence,  
                                  δ 
                                cannot be considered a valid metric on  
                                  X 
                               .

In polytomous KST, we consider a finite set of items denoted by  
                            Q 
                         , and  
                            
                               ( 
                               V 
                               , 
                               ≤ 
                               ) 
                            
                          represents a finite lattice, Unless specified otherwise, in this context,  
                            ⊤ 
                          denotes the top element of  
                            V 
                         , defined as  
                            
                               ⊤ 
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     ⋁ 
                                  
                                  
                                     v 
                                     ∈ 
                                     V 
                                  
                               
                               v 
                            
                         . Similarly,  
                            ⊥ 
                          signifies the bottom element of  
                            V 
                         , defined as  
                            
                               ⊥ 
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     ⋀ 
                                  
                                  
                                     v 
                                     ∈ 
                                     V 
                                  
                               
                               v 
                            
                         . Furthermore,  
                            
                               
                                  V 
                               
                               
                                  Q 
                               
                            
                          represents the family of all mappings from  
                            Q 
                          to  
                            V 
                         . For any non-empty family  
                            
                               K 
                               ⊆ 
                               
                                  
                                     V 
                                  
                                  
                                     Q 
                                  
                               
                            
                          and any  
                            
                               K 
                               , 
                               L 
                               ∈ 
                               K 
                            
                         , a partial order  
                            ⊑ 
                          is defined on  
                            K 
                          as follows:  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     K 
                                     ⊑ 
                                     L 
                                     iff 
                                     K 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        p 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     ≤ 
                                     L 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        p 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     for any 
                                     p 
                                     ∈ 
                                     Q 
                                     . 
                                  
                               
                            
                         We use  
                            
                               K 
                               ⊔ 
                               L 
                            
                          to denote the join of  
                            
                               { 
                               K 
                               , 
                               L 
                               } 
                            
                          if it exists, and  
                            
                               K 
                               ⊓ 
                               L 
                            
                          to denote the meet if it exists. It should be noted that in  
                            
                               
                                  V 
                               
                               
                                  Q 
                               
                            
                         , both  
                            
                               K 
                               ⊔ 
                               L 
                            
                          and  
                            
                               K 
                               ⊓ 
                               L 
                            
                          are operated in an item-wise manner. That is,  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  K 
                                  ⊔ 
                                  L 
                                  ) 
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  p 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               K 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  p 
                                  ) 
                               
                               ∨ 
                               L 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  p 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          and  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  K 
                                  ⊓ 
                                  L 
                                  ) 
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  p 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               K 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  p 
                                  ) 
                               
                               ∧ 
                               L 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  p 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          for any  
                            
                               p 
                               ∈ 
                               Q 
                            
                         . However, in general families  
                            
                               F 
                               ⊆ 
                               
                                  
                                     V 
                                  
                                  
                                     Q 
                                  
                               
                            
                         , the operations of  
                            
                               K 
                               ⊔ 
                               L 
                            
                          and  
                            
                               K 
                               ⊓ 
                               L 
                            
                          may not satisfy the aforementioned principles ( Heller, 2021 ). For the sake of convenience in the remainder of this paper, we introduce the following notations, some of which have been previously introduced in  Ovchinnikov (2002)  and  Sun, Li, He, et al. (2023) .

Notation 2.1 
                            For any  
                                  
                                     K 
                                     , 
                                     L 
                                     ∈ 
                                     
                                        
                                           V 
                                        
                                        
                                           Q 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               , we define the operations  
                                  
                                     K 
                                     ⋓ 
                                     L 
                                     : 
                                     Q 
                                     → 
                                     V 
                                  
                                and  
                                  
                                     K 
                                     ⋒ 
                                     L 
                                     : 
                                     Q 
                                     → 
                                     V 
                                  
                                as follows:  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              K 
                                              ⋓ 
                                              L 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              p 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           = 
                                           K 
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              p 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           ∨ 
                                           L 
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              p 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           for any 
                                           p 
                                           ∈ 
                                           Q 
                                           ; 
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              K 
                                              ⋒ 
                                              L 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              p 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           = 
                                           K 
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              p 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           ∧ 
                                           L 
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              p 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           for any 
                                           p 
                                           ∈ 
                                           Q 
                                           . 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               Consider a non-empty family  
                                  
                                     K 
                                     ⊆ 
                                     
                                        
                                           V 
                                        
                                        
                                           Q 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                and a pair

                                        K 
                                        , 
                                        L 
                                        } 
                                     
                                     ⊆ 
                                     K 
                                  
                               . We define the  interval  
                               
                                  
                                     [ 
                                     K 
                                     , 
                                     L 
                                     ]

                                  K 
                               :

                                              K 
                                              , 
                                              L 
                                              ]

                                              { 
                                              M 
                                              ∈ 
                                              K 
                                              ∣ 
                                              K 
                                              ⋒ 
                                              L 
                                              ⊑ 
                                              M 
                                              ⊑ 
                                              K 
                                              ⋓ 
                                              L 
                                              } 
                                           
                                           .

For any  
                                  
                                     K 
                                     , 
                                     L 
                                     ∈ 
                                     
                                        
                                           V 
                                        
                                        
                                           Q 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               , we define the operations  
                                  
                                     K 
                                     ⋓ 
                                     L 
                                     : 
                                     Q 
                                     → 
                                     V 
                                  
                                and  
                                  
                                     K 
                                     ⋒ 
                                     L 
                                     : 
                                     Q 
                                     → 
                                     V

                                              ( 
                                              K

                                              L 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              p 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           = 
                                           K 
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              p 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           ∨ 
                                           L 
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              p 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           for any 
                                           p

                                           Q

                                              ) 
                                           
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              p

                                           ∧ 
                                           L 
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              p 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           for any 
                                           p 
                                           ∈ 
                                           Q 
                                           . 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               Consider a non-empty family  
                                  
                                     K 
                                     ⊆ 
                                     
                                        
                                           V 
                                        
                                        
                                           Q 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                and a pair  
                                  
                                     
                                        { 
                                        K 
                                        , 
                                        L 
                                        } 
                                     
                                     ⊆ 
                                     K 
                                  
                               . We define the  interval

                                     K 
                                     , 
                                     L 
                                     ]

                                  K

                                              K 
                                              , 
                                              L 
                                              ]

                                              { 
                                              M 
                                              ∈ 
                                              K

                                              K 
                                              ⋒ 
                                              L 
                                              ⊑ 
                                              M 
                                              ⊑ 
                                              K 
                                              ⋓ 
                                              L 
                                              } 
                                           
                                           .

It is easy to verify that if  
                            M 
                          and  
                            N 
                          are within the interval  
                            
                               [ 
                               K 
                               , 
                               L 
                               ] 
                            
                         , then  
                            
                               [ 
                               M 
                               , 
                               N 
                               ] 
                            
                          is a subset of  
                            
                               [ 
                               K 
                               , 
                               L 
                               ] 
                            
                         . The following example illustrate how the interval notation allows us to represent subsets of the structure  
                            K 
                          that lie between two specific elements of  
                            K 
                         .

Example 2.2 
                            Let  
                                  
                                     Q 
                                     = 
                                     
                                        { 
                                        p 
                                        , 
                                        q 
                                        , 
                                        r 
                                        }

                                     V 
                                     = 
                                     
                                        { 
                                        0 
                                        , 
                                        1 
                                        , 
                                        2 
                                        , 
                                        3 
                                        } 
                                     
                                  
                                with numerical order, and  
                                  
                                     K 
                                     = 
                                     
                                        { 
                                        000 
                                        , 
                                        210 
                                        , 
                                        120 
                                        , 
                                        102 
                                        , 
                                        122 
                                        , 
                                        333 
                                        } 
                                     
                                  
                                (see  Fig. 3 ). We can compute the interval  
                                  
                                     [ 
                                     120 
                                     , 
                                     102 
                                     ] 
                                  
                                as  
                                  
                                     
                                        [ 
                                        120 
                                        , 
                                        102 
                                        ] 
                                     
                                     = 
                                     
                                        { 
                                        120 
                                        , 
                                        102 
                                        , 
                                        122 
                                        } 
                                     
                                  
                                based on the operations defined earlier. Specifically, we have  
                                  
                                     120 
                                     ⋒ 
                                     102 
                                     = 
                                     100 
                                  
                                and  
                                  
                                     120 
                                     ⋓ 
                                     102 
                                     = 
                                     122 
                                  
                               . Using the same calculation method, we can obtain similar results such as  
                                  
                                     
                                        [ 
                                        210 
                                        , 
                                        120 
                                        ] 
                                     
                                     = 
                                     
                                        { 
                                        210 
                                        , 
                                        120 
                                        } 
                                     
                                  
                                and  
                                  
                                     
                                        [ 
                                        000 
                                        , 
                                        120 
                                        ] 
                                     
                                     = 
                                     
                                        { 
                                        000 
                                        , 
                                        120 
                                        } 
                                     
                                  
                               .

Let  
                                  
                                     Q 
                                     = 
                                     
                                        { 
                                        p 
                                        , 
                                        q 
                                        , 
                                        r 
                                        } 
                                     
                                  
                                be a set of items,  
                                  
                                     V 
                                     = 
                                     
                                        { 
                                        0 
                                        , 
                                        1 
                                        , 
                                        2 
                                        , 
                                        3 
                                        } 
                                     
                                  
                                with numerical order, and  
                                  
                                     K 
                                     = 
                                     
                                        { 
                                        000 
                                        , 
                                        210 
                                        , 
                                        120 
                                        , 
                                        102 
                                        ,

                                        ,

                                        }
