                         L 
                       more closely within the family  
                         
                            F

                                  0

                      . Furthermore, inspired by  Ovchinnikov (2002) , we make a notable discovery that when  
                         K 
                       and  
                         L 
                       are adjacent elements, their distance  
                         
                            d 
                            
                               ( 
                               K 
                               , 
                               L 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       is equal to 1. Importantly, our proof of this finding diverges from Ovchinnikov’s approach as we employ Eq.  (1)  to measure the values of  
                         ρ 
                       or  
                         δ 
                      , instead of relying on the assumption of a distributive lattice for  
                         V 
                       or  
                         
                            
                               V 
                            
                            
                               Q 
                            
                         
                      . This step is essential in our analysis.  Theorem 3.1  (iv) also serves as a fundamental tool for determining the well-gradedness property in subsequent sections.

Moving forward, we delve into the various manifestations of well-gradedness in polytomous knowledge structures.  Theorem 4.1  extends the conclusion of  Sun, Li, He, et al. (2023)  by establishing that well-gradedness exhibits strong discriminative properties, surpassing the notion that a well-graded polytomous structure is only discriminative. The intriguing findings presented in  Theorem 4.2  and  Example 4.2 , within the context of polytomous knowledge spaces, suggest that even if  
                         V 
                       is a linear order, it does not guarantee the same results as those obtained in classical KST. Lastly,  Theorem 4.3  asserts that discriminative factorial polytomous structures possess the well-gradedness property. It is important to note that relaxing the conditions of  Theorem 4.3  may result in the absence of the well-gradedness property in polytomous structures, as exemplified in  Example 4.4 .

In classic KST, various approaches have been used to study well-graded knowledge structures, such as examining them from the inner and outer fringes or from the atoms. However, defining clear boundaries for inner and outer fringes in the context of polytomous items is challenging. Once a reasonable definition is established, it will facilitate the characterization of well-graded polytomous structures using fringes as a framework. Additionally, it is valuable to explore how well-graded polytomous knowledge structures can be represented by minimal polytomous states.

Currently, our main focus is on polytomous knowledge structures where the response categories form finite lattices. However, the recent work by  Stefanutti et al. (2023)  introduces an intriguing extension to polytomous knowledge structures where the response categories form a partially ordered set. This approach opens up new possibilities, potentially leading to structures beyond traditional knowledge structures, such as polytomous analogs of assessment structures as suggested by  Falmagne (2015)  and characterized by  Heller (2019) . Given these developments, we acknowledge that the metrics used in our current study may need adaptation to be applicable in these broader contexts. As such, further research is necessary to evaluate the applicability of these metrics and to further explore the concept of well-gradedness in these more general polytomous knowledge structures.
